channel.title: The Ark
channel.description: The Ark is the weekly newspaper of Tiburon, Belvedere and Strawberry

item.title: Belvedere budget funds police hiring, lifts staff pay
item.description: The budget includes three years of funding to restore a seventh police position, while the raises follow a study that found Belvedere paying below its Marin neighbors.
item.content:encoded:
Belvedere’s fiscal 2026-2027 budget restores a long-deferred officer position, refills a vacant police sergeant position and raises pay across the city’s workforce after the first salary review in more than 15 years found it paying below its Marin neighbors.

The City Council on June 8 unanimously adopted the $14.49 million budget. The largest in city history, ahead of the July 1 start of the new fiscal year.

The same night, the council approved a two-year contract raising City Manager Robert Zadnik’s salary 12.2% to $267,700 and gave initial approval to three-year contracts for the Police Department and other employees.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister

item.title: Reed Union School District puts $115M bond measure on ballot
item.description: Bond money could go toward replacing portable classrooms, installing air conditioning and upgrading roofs, playgrounds and arts spaces on all three campuses.
item.content:encoded:
The Reed Union School District will ask voters to approve a $115 million bond measure this November to fund facilities upgrades across all three of its campuses.

The district’s board of trustees at its June 8 meeting unanimously approved placing the measure on the Nov. 3 ballot. At the same meeting, the board unanimously approved spending up to $48,050 on a public-information campaign ahead of the election.

Board member Afsaneh Zolfaghari noted that forwarding the bond measure to the ballot was the culmination of four years of discussion and community engagement, including the district’s work to come up with a long-range facilities plan intended to guide building projects at Reed and Bel Aire elementary schools and Del Mar Middle School over the next 10-15 years.

The proposed $115 million bond would charge a tax of $30 per $100,000 of assessed value on properties in the district, which includes Tiburon, Belvedere and east Corte Madera. The district would net about $6.2 million annually. For the median district home assessment of about $2.14 million, the tax would be about $635 annually.

If the bond measure wins the 55% voter approval it needs to pass, the tax would be levied starting in fiscal year 2027-2028 and would run through 2061. The tax amount would likely decrease slightly in the bond’s later years as the district’s debt service decreases.
item.dc:creator: Emily Lavin

item.title: Reed district projects $1.2 mil deficit on $34.5 mil budget
item.description: The Reed Union School District is projecting a $1.2 million deficit in the coming fiscal year as salaries rise and the cost of arts and enrichment programs grows.
item.content:encoded:
The Reed Union School District is projecting a $1.2 million deficit in fiscal 2027 as it provides 3% raises to teachers and staff and plans to spend more than $1 million to close a persistent budget gap for the arts, library and technology programs partially funded by the Foundation for Reed Schools.

The district’s board of trustees voted unanimously at its June 8 meeting to approve the $34.5 million budget for the 2026-2027 fiscal year, which begins July 1.

The budget projects about $33 million in revenue. The district will transfer $510,000 in revenue from tenants at the district-owned Granada campus in east Corte Madera into the general fund and will transfer out $200,000 to its California Employers’ Retiree Benefit Trust, leaving the $1.2 million deficit.

While the district is projecting slight increases in its major sources of revenue, property and parcel taxes, it’s not expecting that to be enough to keep pace with its expenditures next year, forcing it to dip into its general fund to make up the difference.
item.dc:creator: Emily Lavin

item.title: Belvedere grants limited extension for Mallard Pointe redevelopment
item.description: The Belvedere City Council granted the Mallard Pointe developer a shorter extension than it sought, giving it until March 2027 to advance the 40-unit project.
item.content:encoded:
The Belvedere City Council has granted the Mallard Pointe developer more time to complete the steps required to subdivide the property, but far less than it sought. The decision followed a lengthy hearing marked by skeptical questioning from councilmembers and warnings from residents about the risks of demolishing the existing buildings before other pieces of the project are in place.

The council voted unanimously June 8 to extend the tentative map for the 40-unit Mallard Pointe development at 1-22 Mallard Road to March 19, 2027. A tentative map is the city’s preliminary approval to divide a property into building lots, which will let developer Thompson Dorfman Partners sell some of the single-family homes individually — sales it’s counting on to help make the project financially viable. The developer must meet the tentative map’s conditions and record a final map before construction can begin.

The action means the long-debated redevelopment remains alive but under a tighter deadline. The project will replace 22 existing units between City Hall and Belvedere Lagoon with a mix of an apartment building, townhomes, duplexes and single-family homes. Four units must be set aside as affordable for low- and very-low-income households for 55 years.

Thompson Dorfman had asked for a three-year extension, to May 13, 2029. City staff recommended a shorter, two-year extension, to May 13, 2028. The council chose an even shorter time frame, deliberately setting the map to expire the same day as the project’s design review approval.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: A rendering looking north shows homes proposed for the Mallard Pointe development, which would replace 22 units with 40 at 1-22 Mallard Road in Belvedere. The City Council on June 8 extended the project's tentative subdivision map to March 19, 2027. (Sutton Suzuki Architects)

item.title: Summer kickoff on Tiburon's Main Street
item.description: Tiburon's Main Street filled with crowds June 12 for the first Friday Nights on Main festival of the season, timed with the last day of school for Reed Union.
item.content:encoded: Hundreds of people filled lower Main Street in Tiburon on June 12 for the season’s first Friday Nights on Main festival, timed with the last day of school for the Reed Union School District. The Tiburon Peninsula Chamber of Commerce closed the street to traffic for live music from resident Peter Gillespie’s class-rock cover band Tiburon Local Band and children’s activities including face-painting, as well as food and drinks from local restaurants. The chamber will host a second Friday Night on Main on Aug. 21.(Ted McDonnell / For The Ark)
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: Decision due this week on whether Point Tiburon plan can advance
item.description: A Berkeley developer's plan for nine townhomes in the Point Tiburon Plaza parking lot faces a June 18 completeness deadline that could move the project toward approval.
item.content:encoded:
A Berkeley developer’s third bid to build nine townhomes on the Point Tiburon Plaza parking lot faces a town deadline this week that could move the project toward approval.

Tiburon senior planner Kristin Teiche said the town must decide by June 18 whether Black Oak Ventures’ application at 1801 Tiburon Blvd. is complete. The date falls 30 days after the firm’s May 19 resubmission.

A complete finding would advance a 60-day clock toward approval and require the town to prepare a notice of approved application, with any conditions, by July 17, Teiche said. If the application is deemed incomplete, Black Oak would have to resubmit, restarting the process.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Clothes dryer sparks Strawberry house fire; resident and pet forced out
item.description: A clothes dryer started a house fire on Ricardo Road in Strawberry, displacing a resident and their pet, fire officials said.
item.content:encoded:
A clothes dryer ignited a house fire on Ricardo Road in Strawberry over the weekend, displacing a resident and their pet, according to the Southern Marin Fire District.

Fire crews responded about 5 p.m. June 13 and found a garage fully ablaze, with flames spreading into the home and attic, the district said. Firefighters knocked down the fire, searched the home and cut openings to release smoke and heat, bringing it under control in about 15 minutes.

Crews contained the fire by about 5:15 p.m. No injuries were reported.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: Firefighters work to control a house fire on Ricardo Road in Strawberry on June 13. A clothes dryer ignited the blaze, which destroyed the garage and forced a resident and their pet from the home. No one was injured. (via Christian Michael)

item.title: New Business: Nautical clothing brand opens first West Coast outpost on Main Street
item.description: Owner Wayne George said Tiburon's maritime history made it a natural fit for the brand's expansion. He bought the Marblehead store in 2000 from the founding family.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon is roughly 2,700 miles from Marblehead, Massachusetts, but Wayne George says the two towns have a lot in common, including a tradition of and appreciation for maritime culture.

George is the owner of F.L. Woods, a clothing brand with maritime and nautical inspirations that he says has become a mainstay on Massachusetts’ North Shore.

“We’re a tradition in Marblehead,” George says. “Like, people come from all over to come to this old, creaky store that’s been in the same building since the ’30s, and they know that they can always find something great … for Mother’s Day or for Father’s Day or for themselves.”

The store offers an array of men’s and women’s clothing, including quarter-zip sweaters and mariner jackets as well as shorts, hats and belts, plus mementos like postcards and navigational charts.

The Main Street outpost will be overseen by George’s son and Tiburon resident Josh George and Josh’s partner, Marie Bergsund, the granddaughter of former Tiburon Mayor Joan Bergsund.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Tiburon residents Marie Bergsund and Josh George (right) are the managers of F.L. Woods, the maritime-theme clothing store owned by Wayne George (center) that is now open at 21A Main St. in Tiburon. The store is the second after its original in Marblehead, Massachusetts, which opened in 1938. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark_

item.title: Congratulations to the class of 2026
item.description: Members of the Redwood High School class of 2026 held their graduation ceremony June 11, followed by Del Mar Middle School's promotion ceremony June 12.
item.content:encoded: At Redwood’s June 11 commencement, members of the class of 2026 tossed their caps in celebration after receiving their diplomas from Principal Barnaby Payne. Among them were Tiburon residents Stella Belluomini, who plans to attend Clemson University in the fall. The following day, Del Mar Middle School students gathered in the gym ahead of their June 12 promotion ceremony, preparing to make the transition to high school.(Ted McDonnell / For The Ark)
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: Everyday Encounters: A new resident’s life in town centers on tennis
item.description: Ghai, 32, grew up in Chandigarh, India, where his father played tennis for 30 years. He picked up the sport and has coached in the U.S. and the United Arab Emirates.
item.content:encoded:
Akash Ghai’s new job as a tennis professional at the Tiburon Peninsula Club comes with one perk he didn’t have while working in a similar role for the U.S. Tennis Association in Seattle: the area’s temperate climate.

Seattle’s weather proved “quite tricky for tennis,” Ghai says.

“It’s quite rainy for six months, and it gets very cloudy throughout,” he says. “So, I wanted to be in a place which … has a lot more sunny days.”

Ghai, 32, moved to town four months ago and has family nearby in Berkeley. So far, he says, he’s enjoying his new job and what he calls a “very relaxed, laid-back community.”

“It’s been very nice to come to this very relaxed part of California and hope to be here for a while,” he says as he wraps up his lunch break June 10 in Zelinsky Park.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Tiburon resident Akash Ghai is the tennis professional at the Tiburon Peninsula Club. Wrapping up his lunch break June 10 at Zelinsky Park, Ghai says the sport is ‘an amalgamation of life in a short form.’ (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

item.title: Picks of the Week: Car show, boat show, Juneteenth, Concerts in the Park
item.description: The Tiburon Classic Car Show runs 11 a.m.-4 p.m. June 20, followed on June 21 by the Wooden Boat Show at 10 a.m., Juneteenth at 3 p.m. and Super Diamond at 4 p.m.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon Classic CarShow at Shoreline Park

The 26th annual Tiburon Classic and Exotic Car Show runs 11 a.m.-4 p.m. June 20 along the water at Shoreline Park. Local car enthusiasts will be showing off their babies from 1980 and earlier, with the featured marque of Cadillac, including the first Cadillac Formula 1 model. Food and drink will be available for purchase. Ahead of the show, organizer Tom O’Neill will discuss how cars shaped American society, especially teenage culture, after World War II and through the 1950s, ’60s and ’70s. The free talk runs from 6 to 7:30 p.m. June 18 at the Belvedere-Tiburon Library. Both events are free.tiburonclassiccarshow.com.

Neil Diamond tribute first Belvedere concert of summer

After you and Dad hit the Master Mariners Wooden Boat Show at Corinthian Yacht Club from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. on Father’s Day, June 21, then spend time on nearby Main Street for Tiburon’s annual Juneteenth celebration from 3 to 7 p.m., you can rock out at the first of Belvedere’s summer concerts in the park with Neil Diamond tribute act Super Diamond playing all the hits at 4 p.m. at the gazebo in Belvedere Community Park. Opening the show will be a student rock band from Del Mar Middle School. The series continues with Big Crush, playing dance hits and mash-ups, on July 12. The event is free; bring low seating and a picnic.belvedereconcerts.org.
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: Carol Jean Beck
item.description: Carol Jean McCready Beck, age 87, passed away peacefully on Tuesday, June 2.
item.content:encoded:
Carol Jean McCready Beck, age 87, passed away peacefully on Tuesday, June 2, 2026, with her family by her side at the family home in Tiburon, CA, where she lived for the past 59 years.

Born July 23, 1938, she was the only child of Ernest McCready and Selma Sigismund McCready. Carol grew up in the Mission Dolores District of San Francisco and from an early age, had a passion for animals and world travel.

After graduation from Mission High School, Carol was given the opportunity to partake in a circumnavigation voyage around the tip of South America with her father, Ernie, followed by a solo trip to Europe where her father purchased a VW Karmann Ghia that she drove around the countryside before it was shipped back to the US. This whet her appetite for a lifetime of travel. Upon returning to the states, Carol was employed as a telephone switchboard operator for Pac Bell.

Carol met Bill Beck in the summer of 1960, and they were married on December 8th, 1962. Shortly after they wed, they moved to Tiburon, CA, where they built their first home, raised their three sons and enjoyed an incredible run of 63 years of marriage. This remains the family home today.

Carol and Bill built a second home in Sea Ranch, CA, in 1991 and enjoyed many happy times there on the coast with family and friends. It became a family tradition to spend each Thanksgiving there.

Carol was a real history buff, with a voracious curiosity about the world, and loved sharing her knowledge and insights so much that she became a licensed San Francisco Bay Area Tour Guide for over a decade. Throughout the years, Carol had the opportunity and privilege to travel to more than 90 different countries and territories, both with her husband Bill, and also with close girlfriends.

In addition to her travels, Carol was passionate about animals, especially the golden retriever, having rescued many throughout her lifetime.

Carol was a minimalist and could often be heard using the phrase, “Less is More!” She left this imprint on her children and grandchildren.

Carol is survived by her husband, William (Bill) Beck; her three sons, Raymond (Colleen), Roger, and Ronald (Megan); and four grandchildren, Bailey, Peyton, Colby, and Calvin.

In lieu of flowers, please consider a donation to one of the following organizations that Carol supported throughout her lifetime: Doctors Without Borders, EIN: 13-3433452, 333 Seventh Avenue, 2nd Floor, New York, NY...
item.dc:creator: Reader submitted

item.title: Harriet Lee Humphrey
item.description: Longtime Belvedere-Tiburon, CA resident Harriet Lee Humphrey, known as Lee, passed away at age 91 on May 30, 2026, in San Rafael, CA.
item.content:encoded:
Longtime Belvedere-Tiburon, CA resident Harriet Lee Humphrey, known as Lee, passed away at age 91 on May 30, 2026, in San Rafael, CA.

Born in Arlington, Virginia in 1934 to Lillian Frances and Harry James Humphrey, Lee graduated from Washington & Lee High School and George Washington University (GW) with a BA in history and religion. Though she lived in California for more than 60 years, she always considered herself a “Washingtonian,” maintaining many of the childhood and college friendships she made while in Virginia and the District of Columbia.

After graduating from GW, Lee married Joseph Barish in Washington, D.C., where they welcomed their first daughter, Lisa, in 1956. Joe’s work took the family to the Bay Area in 1959 and in 1961, they added twin girls, Amy and Andrea, to the family. In 1966, they moved back to D.C. for two years for Joe’s work at the Capitol and then returned to California in 1968, settling in Belvedere for almost two decades.

A lifelong learner, passionate about art, history and music, Lee earned her master’s degree in arts administration at Golden Gate University. She put her passion to work in management positions at The Mexican Museum, Young Audiences and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (SFMOMA), where she was director of volunteers. She also served as a docent at SFMOMA for more than a decade. More recently, she kept sharp and active taking courses on history, literature and politics through Osher Lifelong Learning, the JCC and Dominican University. She also volunteered at the Corner Books store at the Belvedere-Tiburon Library, where she enjoyed engaging with her community.

More than anything, Lee loved being near, by and in the water – a strong swimmer, she was always the first person in the pool, ocean, lake or bay, no matter what the temperature. Though she loved to drive, she was also an avid walker who could be seen frequenting the Old Rail Trail along Richardson Bay and the path around Belvedere Lagoon.

In her daughters and grandchildren, she has deeply embedded her love of learning, reading, PBS Masterpiece Mystery, crossword puzzles, handwritten thank you notes, art and music, cooking and good food, travel and adventure and all things holiday.

Lee is survived by her daughters: Lisa Slater of San Francisco, Amy Barish of Oakland and Andrea Barish (Tom Ross) of Belvedere; grandsons Gabriel Slater (Serena Patel) and William Slater (Electa Willander) and three nieces an...
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: Lois Mae Moody
item.description: Lois Mae Moody passed away at 98 rather unexpectedly on Friday evening. April 17th, 2026.
item.content:encoded:
Lois Mae Moody passed away at 98 rather unexpectedly on Friday evening. April 17th, 2026. On her last day, she enjoyed her weekly Friday lunch with her friends at the San Francisco Yacht Club.

Lois was born in San Francisco to Irving and Adeline Moulin on February 10, 1928. She attended Lincoln High School and later graduated from the University of California at Berkeley, where she met her future husband, Bruce Moody.

Lois and Bruce married in 1953 and lived in San Francisco before moving to Belvedere in 1956. In 1959, they built their family home in Tiburon, where they spent the rest of their lives together. They raised their 3 children —Tod, Duncan and Janet — and delighted in their six grandchildren: Garrett, Suzie, James, Alex, Melissa, and Matt.

Lois had a deep love of the outdoors and enjoyed playing tennis, hunting, hiking, boating, and camping. On hikes she was known for identifying plants and animals by their Latin names, sharing her curiosity and knowledge with others. Her garden was filled with native plants that she nurtured from seeds to maturity.

She was adventurous in the kitchen and loved trying out new recipes on her guests, with much success. Lois was a talented seamstress, always choosing her favorite designers’ patterns and fine fabrics to create her elegant masterpieces.

Lois loved protecting plants and animals and had several dogs and cats, a tortoise and even a Castilian donkey. She served as a docent at the Audubon Canyon Ranch, where she taught children about egrets and other animals there.

In retirement, Lois and Bruce travelled the world from the jungles of India, Africa and the Amazon to the mountains of Peru.

Lois and Bruce were San Francisco Yacht Club members for 56 years. They loved cruising on the Contessa, a 1923 Blanchard 36 cruiser, and the Lone Ranger, a Ranger 33 sailboat, sailing with friends and family. Their adventures took them to places including the Canal du Midi, the turquoise coast of Turkey, Croatia, British Columbia, and the San Joaquin Delta.

Lois is predeceased by her husband, Bruce Moody, who died on November 17th, 2023, and her brother Don Moulin. Lois is survived by all her children, grandchildren and their partners and her sister-in-law Barbara Moulin.
item.dc:creator: Reader submitted

item.title: Dr. James E. Shepard
item.description: Dr. James E. Shepard, M.D., F.A.C.P., passed away peacefully on May 19, 2026, at the age of 92.
item.content:encoded:
Dr. James E. Shepard, M.D., F.A.C.P., beloved husband, father, grandfather, physician, traveler, and lifelong student of the world, passed away peacefully on May 19, 2026, at the age of 92.

Born in Franklin, NH, Jim combined a sharp intellect, deep curiosity, and gentle humor that stayed with him throughout his life. He shared 67 years of marriage with the love of his life, Sally-Jean Shepard, a partnership marked by enduring affection, shared adventures, and countless journeys together.

Dr. Shepard graduated from Tilton School, Tilton, NH; Wesleyan University, Middletown, CT; and Weill Cornell Medical College, NYC. His medical training began at Mary Bassett Hospital in Cooperstown, NY. He then served two years as an Army physician at Ft. Story, Virginia Beach, VA. Following his military service, he completed additional medical training at the University of California at San Francisco and went on to build a distinguished career in nephrology and internal medicine in Marin County.

Over more than six decades, Dr. Shepard became a respected leader in the medical community, serving as Chief of Medicine and Chairman of the Intensive Care Unit at Marin General Hospital. He helped establish one of the earliest dialysis programs in California. He was board certified in both internal medicine and nephrology. He was elected a Fellow of the American College of Physicians (F.A.C.P.) and later served as a clinical faculty member affiliated with UCSF. Following his years in clinical practice, he continued working as a medical consultant and expert witness well into his later years.

Outside of medicine, Jim loved travel, conversation, golf, tennis, and learning. He and Sally explored the world together for decades; maps in hand, curiosity leading the way. He was an Emeritus Life Member of the Mill Valley Tennis Club, where he enjoyed both the game and the friendships surrounding it.

Dr. Shepard is survived by his wife, Sally-Jean Shepard; daughters Sandy Shepard Wolfram and Elisabeth Shepard; granddaughter Leann McFalls; and great-grandsons Caleb and Cody McFalls.

In remembrance of Jim’s lifelong generosity and concern for others, the family suggests donations to local food banks in lieu of flowers.
item.dc:creator: Reader submitted

item.title: Supervisors greenlight Strawberry seminary redevelopment after years of conflict
item.description: The project calls for 336 homes on the hilltop campus, including 70 below-market-rate units, plus a 150-unit senior care facility and a graduate school.
item.content:encoded:
The former Golden Gate Baptist Theological Seminary campus in Strawberry will be redeveloped into hundreds of homes, a senior-care facility and a graduate school, capping more than a decade of debate over the future of one of Southern Marin’s last large redevelopment sites.

The Marin County Board of Supervisors approved the project unanimously at its June 9 meeting after several hours of staff presentations. Public comment drew 26 speakers, most of whom criticized the proposal.

The board adopted the staff recommendation in full, certifying the project’s environmental review and approving its master plan, design and permits on a motion by Supervisor Stephanie Moulton-Peters, whose District 3 covers Southern Marin. It denied a related amendment to the Strawberry Community Plan, which staff said would not reduce the housing because developer North Coast Land Holdings can rely on state density-bonus law instead.

The approval clears North Coast to move forward with its building-permit application. The dispute had pitted Strawberry neighbors against the developer, with the county in the middle.

Before the Planning Commission’s March 2 hearing, North Coast reached an agreement with the Seminary Neighborhood Association, which had long opposed the development. On March 30 the commission recommended approval, but some residents said they felt left out.

In her presentation to the board, Principal Planner Michelle Levenson called the proposal “another chapter” in the history of the Strawberry seminary. She said the process had drawn substantial input from the public and stakeholders, including more than 50 meetings as part of the Seminary Tomorrow process.

Staff said the project is shaped by California housing laws that sharply limit the county’s ability to deny or shrink the proposal. The environmental impact report found three significant and unavoidable effects: greenhouse-gas emissions, temporary construction noise and transportation effects tied to vehicle miles traveled. Most other impacts could be mitigated.

The agreement between the developer and the neighborhood association added conditions limiting academic use to graduate, postgraduate and research fields, capping commuting students at 325 unless additional students live on site, and restricting the daycare and fitness center primarily to residents and on-site users. County staff and an attorney for North Coast said the agreement is enforceable as a condi...
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: A photo illustration depicts buildings proposed for the former Golden Gate Baptist Theological Seminary campus, a 127-acre site in Strawberry overlooking San Francisco Bay. The Marin County Board of Supervisors approved the redevelopment June 9. (Mark Cavagnero Associates Architects)

item.title: Tiburon boosts parks funding in draft $28.38 million budget
item.description: Parents for Parks and Rec pushed the council for more funding, saying the town's five-year plan includes little money for meaningful new park improvements.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon officials directed staff to put an additional $1.5 million toward parks in next year’s budget, responding to residents who pressed the town for a firmer commitment to its recreational amenities.

The Town Council gave the direction June 3 as it reviewed a draft $28.38 million budget for fiscal year 2026-2027. The council took no final action, and its directives will trim that figure to about $27.88 million in the revised budget it is scheduled to adopt June 17. Councilmember Holli Thier was absent.

The $1.5 million combines $1 million transferred from the town’s general-fund reserves with $500,000 freed by dropping a planned repair of the Elephant Rock fishing pier. Because the reserve money moves between town accounts rather than adding to spending, the budget total falls rather than rises. That $1.5 million is on top of $1 million already set to move into the parks fund in fiscal 2027, which begins July 1.

Parks and their costs ran through much of the discussion, and councilmembers also signaled openness to creating a finance committee to guide the town’s long-term revenue planning.

The draft’s $28.38 million total includes $19.13 million in day-to-day operating expenditures, nearly $8 million for infrastructure upgrades under the town’s capital-improvement program and $1.25 million in debt service. Dropping the $500,000 pier project will lower the revised budget returning June 17 to about $27.88 million and its associated capital-improvement program to roughly $7.5 million, Town Manager Greg Chanis said after the meeting.

Operating expenditures run just under the $19.15 million in revenue, a $21,642 surplus. Expenditures and revenue are each projected to rise 4.79% from the current fiscal 2026 budget.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Neighbors lose bid to roll back pickleball hours at Tiburon Peninsula Club
item.description: Public hearings over pickleball at the private club have stretched nearly three years, rooted in a 2019 dispute over noise and unpermitted play.
item.content:encoded:
Expanded pickleball hours at the Tiburon Peninsula Club are here to stay after the Town Council rejected an appeal from neighbors who complained about the sport’s noise and its impact on their homes and lives.

Councilmembers spent nearly two hours hearing from club members and opposing neighbors before voting 2-1 at their June 3 meeting to uphold the Planning Commission’s decision to expand playing hours to seven days a week and allow longer hours in spring and summer. The vote rejected an appeal from Mar West Street resident Eric Pifer.

Vice Mayor Isaac Nikfar and Councilmember Jack Ryan voted in favor, while Mayor Jon Welner cast the dissenting vote, siding with neighbors in saying “there is a qualitative difference with the kind of noise that you get in pickleball” compared with other nearby sounds.

As a result of the council’s vote, the private club will have expanded pickleball hours of 9 a.m.-6:30 p.m. Mondays and Wednesdays through Saturdays from March 1 to Oct. 31, and 9 a.m.-5 p.m. those days from November through February. Mondays had previously been off limits.

Play will also be allowed 10 a.m.-4:30 p.m. Sundays year-round, a 2½-hour increase, and 9 a.m.-4 p.m. Tuesdays year-round, a one-hour reduction. Commissioners trimmed the Tuesday hours at their March 25 hearing to give neighbors some respite from the noise.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Taco trouble: A two-week craze sparked debate over health rules, entrepreneurship and what residents want
item.description: A Mexican food stand that drew long lines and rave reviews on Tiburon Boulevard was shut down after two weeks over health-code violations including no permit and no refrigeration.
item.content:encoded:
Taqueria El Chachoset up shop at Tiburon Boulevard’s inbound intersection with Greenwood Cove Drive, next to Westminster Presbyterian Church, in mid-May.It quickly built a social-media following online, thanks to its street-food classics: tacos de asada, burritos al pastor and quesadillas con chorizo.

However, as customers lined up, complaints also came in. The Marin County Sheriff’s Office received seven calls about the stand between May 15 and May 31, with callers questioning its legality and safety practices.

County health officials, with support from the Sheriff’s Office, on May 29 confiscated what one official described as “hundreds of pounds of food” amid several health-code violations at the Tiburon Boulevard site: no permit, no handwashing stations, no refrigeration and no clear source for the food or details on how it was prepared.

Locals on Nextdoor were up in arms. A post about the stand’s May 29 shutdown drew 216 reactions and 141 comments, with many supporting the vendor and some raising concerns about health standards.

“I knew this would happen. Was just waiting for a Karen or Ken to freak out,” Reedlands resident Maryam Fooman said in a comment. “Let them be. They are just trying to make a living. You don’t feel like it’s safe to eat there, then don’t. Shame on you.”

On May 30, the stand reappeared on the other side of Tiburon Boulevard near the 76 gas station.

The stand was shut down for good May 31, though a handmade cardboard sign remained posted at its original location near the church: “The people want tacos.”

“We’re going to look (for) the way to move forward and be there in Tiburon as well,”owner MiguelLopez, a 20-year-old L.A. resident, said in a June 4 interview conducted in both English and Spanish.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Taqueria El Chacho in its two-week presence at the corner of Tiburon Boulevard and Greenwood Cove Drive, seen here May 27, drew rave reviews and long lines from locals looking to try their food. The street-food stand was shut down twice following complaints over unpermitted food selling, which led to an outcry on local forums like Nextdoor. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

After Taqueria El Chacho was shut down May 31 at the Tiburon Boulevard-Greenwood Cove Drive intersection near the 76 gas station, devoted fans put up a message of support for the taqueria, saying ‘the people want tacos,’ at its original location near Westminster Presbyterian Church, seen here June 1. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

item.title: Peninsula kicks off summer with street fest — then car show, boat show, Juneteenth and free concert
item.description: Friday Nights on Main is June 12, the annual Tiburon Classic Car Show is June 20, then June 21 is the wooden boat show, Juneteenth and Belvedere Concert in the Park.
item.content:encoded:
The peninsula’s summer season kicks off over the next two weekends, starting with Friday Night on Main on June 12, when lower Main Street will close to traffic for an evening of live music, children’s activities and outdoor dining.

The festivities continue Father’s Day weekend with the Tiburon Classic and Exotic Car Show, a wooden boat show, a Juneteenth celebration on Main Street and a concert in Belvedere Community Park.

The Tiburon Peninsula Chamber of Commerce will spark the summer with the first of two Friday Nights on Main, from 5:30 to 8:30 p.m. June 12. The event falls on the last day of school for the Reed Union School District.Lower Main Street will be closed to traffic for live music, children’s activities and alfresco food and drinks.

The following weekend packs in four events: a car show, a wooden boat show, a Juneteenth celebration and a Neil Diamond tribute concert.

On June 20, Tiburon will rev up for the 26th annual Tiburon Classic and Exotic Car Show, from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. at Shoreline Park.The big draw this year will be Cadillac’s first Formula 1 race car.

June 21 is Father’s Day, and three events fill the day.

The Corinthian Yacht Club will host its annual Master Mariners Wooden Boat Show from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. at 43 Main St.Two historic boats from Spaulding Marine Center will be among those on display.

Tiburon’s Diversity Inclusion Task Force will celebrate Juneteenth from 3 to 7 p.m. on lower Main Street. The celebration will feature live jazz, hip-hop, poetry readings and speakers. Hip-Hop For Change, an Oakland-based nonprofit that works with marginalized youth to reclaim the roots of hip-hop culture, will present poetry and an interactive performance.

The peninsula will wrap up the weekend with a June 21 concert by Super Diamond, a Neil Diamond tribute band, at 4 p.m. at Belvedere Community Park. The opening act will be a local rock band from Del Mar Middle School.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
